title: The Poppy's Promise: Medicine, Crisis, and Harm Reduction
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain why opioids are this season's hardest story and how it is told here.
Subject: Episode introduction | Run time: about 3 minutes

[WELCOME] Welcome back to Better Vice Club, Season 3, The Forbidden Leaf. This is Anthony McDonald. This is the hardest episode in the whole series, and the most important. It is about the opium poppy, the medicine that became the deadliest drug crisis in American history.

[HOOK] Read this part carefully, because it could matter to someone you love. This is a public-health and history class. It is never a how-to. Opioids are extraordinarily dangerous, and today the supply is often contaminated with fentanyl, a synthetic opioid so potent that a tiny amount can kill. If you ever think someone is overdosing, going limp, breathing slowly, blue lips, call 911 immediately. A medication called naloxone, sold as Narcan, can reverse an opioid overdose and save a life, and it is available without a prescription at most pharmacies. For help with substance use, the SAMHSA helpline is free, confidential, and open 24 hours: 1-800-662-4357. Now the scale of what we are studying. From 1999 to 2021, roughly 645,000 people in the United States died of opioid overdose (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2023). We will treat every one of those as a person, not a statistic.

[TEACH] We read opioids through four lenses. Geography asks where the poppy grows and how fentanyl follows a chemical supply chain instead. Social studies follows the poppy from an ancient joy plant to the Opium Wars to the documented deception of Purdue Pharma. Economics asks who profited from this crisis and who paid, and shows why treatment and harm reduction cost far less than punishment. And English language arts studies the language of the crisis, from a Chinese official's letter to a modern marketing campaign. Each lesson is short and ends with a quick question.

[REMEMBER] Hold this, and hold it gently. The opioid story is about a real medicine, real pain, real profit, and real loss on a scale that is hard to take in. The hopeful part is also true: overdoses can be reversed with naloxone, and treatment works. This episode honors the lives lost and points toward what actually helps.

[CHECK] Quick check. What medication can reverse an opioid overdose, and what should you do first if you think someone is overdosing? [Beat] Naloxone, sold as Narcan, can reverse it, and you should call 911 immediately (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2023).

## Key Takeaways
- This is a public-health and history class on the opioid crisis, never a how-to or an endorsement.
- If someone may be overdosing, call 911; naloxone (Narcan) can reverse an opioid overdose and is widely available. Help: SAMHSA 1-800-662-4357.
- From 1999 to 2021, roughly 645,000 people in the U.S. died of opioid overdose (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2023).
- The episode honors those lives and emphasizes that treatment and harm reduction work.

## Sources
- Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. (2023). Understanding the opioid overdose epidemic. https://www.cdc.gov/opioids/

title: Where the Poppy Grows
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain what the opium poppy needs and why production concentrates where government control is weak.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we opened the opioid episode. Two quick questions.
One: what should you do first if you think someone is overdosing? [Beat] Call 911; naloxone can reverse an opioid overdose (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2023).
Two: roughly how many Americans died of opioid overdose from 1999 to 2021? [Beat] About 645,000 (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2023).

[HOOK] The opium poppy is a real crop with real growing needs, like coffee or grapes. But its map is different from any other plant in this series, because where it is grown for drugs depends less on the best soil and more on where no government is watching.

[TEACH] The plant is Papaver somniferum, and it likes a temperate to subtropical climate, modest rainfall of about 300 to 500 millimeters, well-drained alkaline soil, and a frost-free season of at least 120 days (Kapoor, 1995). Harvesting opium is skilled, labor-intensive hand work. Plenty of regions could grow it. But illicit production clusters in just two: the Golden Triangle of Myanmar, Laos, and Thailand, and the Golden Crescent of Afghanistan, Pakistan, and Iran (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).
Here is the geographic lesson, and it is the key one. These regions share more than climate. They share weak or absent central government control. Illicit production needs the shelter of limited enforcement, so it settles in mountainous, hard-to-police areas where the state is far away. And the map is not fixed. In 2023, a Taliban ban cut Afghan opium production by about 95 percent, so production simply shifted, and Myanmar became the world's largest producer (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023). Demand did not fall. The crop just moved to the next ungoverned space.

[PICTURE] Look at the map on this lesson. Two regions glow: the Golden Crescent in the high, arid mountains of Central Asia, and the Golden Triangle in the rugged highlands of Southeast Asia. Both are places where the map says one country but the ground answers to no central state. The poppy grows where the law cannot reach.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the opium poppy (Papaver somniferum) grows in temperate to subtropical climates, but illicit production concentrates in the Golden Triangle and Golden Crescent because those regions combine suitable climate with weak central government control, and when Afghanistan's production fell 95 percent in 2023, it simply shifted to Myanmar (Kapoor, 1995; United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023). Production follows the absence of the state.

[CHECK] Quick check. Beyond climate, what do the Golden Triangle and Golden Crescent share? [Beat] Weak or absent central government control, which gives illicit production the shelter of limited enforcement (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).

## Key Takeaways
- The opium poppy (Papaver somniferum) needs a temperate to subtropical climate and well-drained soil (Kapoor, 1995).
- Illicit production concentrates in the Golden Triangle and the Golden Crescent (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).
- Those regions share weak central government control, because illicit growing needs the shelter of limited enforcement.
- A 2023 Taliban ban cut Afghan production about 95 percent, shifting the crop to Myanmar while demand held steady.

## Sources
- Kapoor, L. D. (1995). Opium poppy: Botany, chemistry, and pharmacology. Haworth Press.
- United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime. (2023). World drug report 2023. https://www.unodc.org

title: The Fentanyl Map
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how fentanyl's geography differs from a crop and why that makes it harder to stop.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we mapped the poppy. Two quick questions.
One: what do the Golden Triangle and Golden Crescent share beyond climate? [Beat] Weak central government control (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).
Two: what happened when Afghanistan's production fell 95 percent? [Beat] It shifted to Myanmar; demand held steady (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).

[HOOK] For most of history, opioids came from a field. Today, the deadliest one does not. Fentanyl is made in a laboratory, and that single fact has rewritten the entire geography of the crisis, and made it far harder to stop.

[TEACH] Fentanyl is a synthetic opioid, 50 to 100 times more potent than morphine, and it is the main driver of overdose deaths today (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2023). Because it is synthetic, its map is industrial, not agricultural. The chemical precursors are produced mostly in China, with its large industrial chemical capacity. The synthesis happens mostly in Mexico, in cartel laboratory networks that use existing trafficking routes. Then it moves north, mostly across the land border, into the same distribution networks that already existed (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).
This is the key insight, and it is sobering. You can try to fight a plant-based drug by eradicating crops. You cannot eradicate a chemical reaction. Fentanyl follows chemical supply chains and criminal infrastructure, not soil and climate, so crop-eradication strategies do almost nothing against it. And criminalization itself creates the staggering markup: a kilogram that costs a few thousand dollars to produce can be worth more than a million dollars on the street. The huge profit margin is not a feature of the chemical. It is a feature of the law that bans it.

[PICTURE] Look at the map on this lesson. Picture a chemical plant in China, a hidden lab in Mexico, and a line crossing the United States border, then fanning out into the hardest-hit communities. There is no field anywhere on this map. The geography of the deadliest opioid is a supply chain, not a harvest.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: fentanyl is a synthetic opioid 50 to 100 times stronger than morphine, and its geography is industrial, precursors from China, synthesis in Mexico, transit across the U.S. border, which makes crop eradication useless, while criminalization creates an enormous price markup (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2023; United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023). It follows chemistry, not climate.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why is fentanyl harder to disrupt than plant-based opioids? [Beat] Its geography is industrial, following chemical supply chains and criminal networks rather than soil and climate, so crop eradication does not work (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).

## Key Takeaways
- Fentanyl is a synthetic opioid 50 to 100 times more potent than morphine, the main driver of overdose deaths today (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2023).
- Its geography is industrial: precursors from China, synthesis in Mexico, transit across the U.S. border.
- Because it follows chemistry, not climate, crop eradication does almost nothing against it.
- Criminalization creates a huge price markup, from a few thousand dollars a kilogram to over a million on the street.

## Sources
- Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. (2023). Understanding the opioid overdose epidemic. https://www.cdc.gov/opioids/
- United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime. (2023). World drug report 2023. https://www.unodc.org

title: Where the Harm Concentrated
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain why the opioid crisis hit some American regions hardest.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we mapped fentanyl. Two quick questions.
One: where is fentanyl mostly synthesized? [Beat] In Mexico, from precursors made mostly in China (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).
Two: why does crop eradication not work against fentanyl? [Beat] Its geography is industrial, not agricultural (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).

[HOOK] The opioid crisis did not fall evenly across America. It carved out a clear map, and that map lines up almost exactly with places where the economy had already collapsed. The harm was not random. It followed despair.

[TEACH] The states with the highest overdose death rates include West Virginia, Ohio, Kentucky, Tennessee, and Pennsylvania (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2023). Look at what they share. Many are in Appalachia, in coalfield communities that lost their jobs when mining declined. Many are in the Rust Belt, where steel and manufacturing left. And many are rural, with limited healthcare and long distances to any treatment. Economists Anne Case and Angus Deaton named this pattern deaths of despair, a rise in deaths from drugs, alcohol, and suicide in communities hollowed out by economic decline (Case & Deaton, 2020).
Now the part that turns geography into accusation. Purdue Pharma tracked prescription data by location and concentrated its OxyContin marketing in exactly these places: economically distressed rural areas with high rates of physical-labor injury and few healthcare alternatives (Keefe, 2021). The crisis did not simply happen to these communities. They were targeted. Indiana is in this story too. Its southern counties share Appalachian economic geography, and the town of Austin, in Scott County, became the site of a major HIV outbreak in 2015 tied to injectable opioid use, which we will return to in the economics lessons.

[PICTURE] Picture a map of the United States with the overdose deaths shaded dark. The darkest band runs through the Appalachian mountains and the old industrial Midwest, the same places where the mines closed and the factories left. Now lay Purdue's marketing map on top. They match. The pain was where the jobs used to be.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the opioid crisis concentrated in Appalachia and the Rust Belt, rural communities of economic decline that Case and Deaton call deaths of despair, and Purdue Pharma deliberately targeted its OxyContin marketing at exactly these distressed areas (Case & Deaton, 2020; Keefe, 2021). The harm followed the economic collapse, and was aimed there.

[CHECK] Quick check. What are deaths of despair, and how do they relate to opioid geography? [Beat] A rise in deaths from drugs, alcohol, and suicide in economically declining communities, which is exactly where the opioid crisis hit hardest (Case & Deaton, 2020).

## Key Takeaways
- The highest overdose death rates concentrated in Appalachia and the Rust Belt, including West Virginia, Ohio, and Kentucky (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2023).
- These areas share post-industrial economic decline and limited rural healthcare access.
- Case and Deaton call the broader pattern deaths of despair (Case & Deaton, 2020).
- Purdue Pharma tracked prescription data and targeted its marketing at exactly these distressed communities (Keefe, 2021).

## Sources
- Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. (2023). Understanding the opioid overdose epidemic. https://www.cdc.gov/opioids/
- Case, A., & Deaton, A. (2020). Deaths of despair and the future of capitalism. Princeton University Press.
- Keefe, P. R. (2021). Empire of pain: The secret history of the Sackler dynasty. Doubleday.

title: From Joy Plant to Medicine
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can trace the poppy from ancient medicine to the first repeated false promises of a "non-addictive" opioid.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we mapped where the harm concentrated. Two quick questions.
One: what are deaths of despair? [Beat] A rise in deaths from drugs, alcohol, and suicide in communities hollowed out by economic decline (Case & Deaton, 2020).
Two: how did Purdue Pharma decide where to push its marketing? [Beat] It tracked prescription data by location and concentrated on economically distressed rural areas with few healthcare alternatives (Keefe, 2021).

[HOOK] The opioid story does not begin in a boardroom. It begins in a field, more than five thousand years ago, with a plant that people loved long before anyone understood what it could cost them. And from the very start, that plant carried a promise. The trouble is that the promise kept getting told as a lie.

[TEACH] Start at the beginning. Around 3400 BCE, in ancient Mesopotamia, the Sumerians cultivated the opium poppy and gave it a name: hul gil, the joy plant (Booth, 1996). The name tells you how they saw it, as a source of pleasure and relief, something powerful and good. Centuries later, around 1550 BCE, the Egyptian Ebers Papyrus, one of the oldest medical texts we have, documented opium as medicine (Booth, 1996). For thousands of years, this was the whole story: a plant, a resin, a remedy passed down through cultures.
Then came the pharmaceutical turn. In 1804, a German pharmacist named Friedrich Serturner isolated the single active compound inside opium and named it morphine, after Morpheus, the Greek god of dreams (Courtwright, 2001). This was a genuine scientific achievement, one of the first times a specific molecule was pulled out of a plant and identified as the active agent. But concentration changes everything. A purified molecule is far stronger than the plant it came from.
You can see what that meant in the American Civil War. Morphine was used heavily for the pain of battlefield wounds, and afterward roughly 400,000 veterans were left dependent on it, a condition people at the time called soldier's disease (Courtwright, 2001). For the first time, opioid addiction was a mass-scale American health problem. And notice what it revealed: these were veterans who got morphine for real injuries. Addiction followed exposure, not weakness of character.
Now the part that becomes a pattern. In 1898, the Bayer company began marketing heroin as a non-addictive substitute for morphine (Booth, 1996). That claim was false. Heroin is morphine processed for faster onset, and it is intensely addictive. But sit with the shape of the lie, because you will see it again and again. A new opioid arrives, sold as the safe answer to the addiction caused by the last one. The joy plant gave us morphine. Morphine gave us dependence. Heroin was sold as the cure for that dependence, and it was not. The promise of a safe opioid is one of the oldest false promises in medicine, and it would be made again, almost a century later, by a company called Purdue.

[PICTURE] Picture a line of hands passing the same plant forward across five thousand years. A Sumerian scratching hul gil into clay. An Egyptian scribe writing it into a medical scroll. A chemist in 1804 holding a white powder far stronger than the flower. A Civil War surgeon with a needle. And a company in 1898 printing the word "non-addictive" on a heroin label. Same molecule family, same promise, told again and again, and each time the promise broke.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the poppy was cultivated as medicine for thousands of years, called hul gil by the Sumerians around 3400 BCE, then concentrated into morphine by Serturner in 1804, which left roughly 400,000 dependent Civil War veterans with soldier's disease, and in 1898 Bayer falsely marketed heroin as a non-addictive substitute for morphine (Booth, 1996; Courtwright, 2001). The recurring theme: each new opioid was sold as the safe answer to the last one's addiction, and the promise was always false.

[CHECK] Quick check. What is the recurring false promise that runs through opioid history? [Beat] That a new opioid is the safe, non-addictive answer to the addiction caused by the opioid before it, a promise made for heroin in 1898 and broken every time (Booth, 1996).

## Key Takeaways
- The Sumerians cultivated the poppy around 3400 BCE and called it hul gil, the joy plant; the Egyptian Ebers Papyrus documented opium as medicine around 1550 BCE (Booth, 1996).
- Friedrich Serturner isolated morphine in 1804, the first pharmaceutical isolation of a plant compound; concentration made it far stronger (Courtwright, 2001).
- Heavy Civil War use of morphine left roughly 400,000 dependent veterans, a pattern called soldier's disease, showing addiction follows exposure, not weakness (Courtwright, 2001).
- In 1898 Bayer falsely marketed heroin as a non-addictive substitute for morphine, the first corporate version of a promise that kept repeating (Booth, 1996).

## Sources
- Booth, M. (1996). Opium: A history. St. Martin's Press.
- Courtwright, D. T. (2001). Dark paradise: A history of opiate addiction in America. Harvard University Press.

title: The Opium Wars
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how Britain forced the opium trade on China to protect its own profits.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we traced the poppy from joy plant to medicine. Two quick questions.
One: what did the Sumerian name hul gil mean? [Beat] The joy plant, the name the Sumerians gave the opium poppy around 3400 BCE (Booth, 1996).
Two: what false claim did Bayer make about heroin in 1898? [Beat] That it was a non-addictive substitute for morphine, which was not true (Booth, 1996).

[HOOK] We usually think of drug wars as governments fighting to stop drugs. Here is a war fought to force them in. In the 1800s, the most powerful empire on earth went to war twice, not to end the opium trade, but to keep it open against a country that was trying to shut it down. To understand why, follow the money.

[TEACH] Start with the trade problem. By the early 1800s, Britain was buying enormous quantities of Chinese tea, silk, and porcelain, and paying for them in silver (Lovell, 2011). The problem was that China wanted very little in return. British goods held little interest there, so silver flowed out of Britain and into China and did not come back. That is a trade deficit, and it was draining Britain's reserves.
The British East India Company found a way to reverse it. The Company grew opium in Bengal, in India, and sold it into China through intermediary traders (Lovell, 2011). Now the silver flowed the other way. Chinese buyers paid for opium, the Company captured the profit, and the trade deficit flipped in Britain's favor. The economics worked beautifully for the empire. The human cost landed entirely on China.
And the cost was severe. Demand exploded into millions of Chinese opium users, with deep social disruption, families and communities pulled apart (Lovell, 2011). China's government grew alarmed and moved to stop the trade. In 1839, a Chinese official named Lin Zexu confiscated and destroyed more than 1,200 tons of opium and demanded that Britain end the trade (Lovell, 2011).
Here is the turning point. Britain could have accepted China's right to regulate what came into its own country. Instead, Britain went to war to protect the profits. The First Opium War ran from 1839 to 1842, and the Second from 1856 to 1860 (Lovell, 2011). China lost both. The resulting treaties forced China to accept the opium trade, pay war reparations, open additional ports to foreign trade, and cede Hong Kong to Britain (Lovell, 2011). An empire used military force to make a sovereign nation keep buying a drug, because stopping the drug would have cost the empire money.
Now connect it to the larger theme of this whole season. In the cannabis episode, we saw drug policy driven by politics rather than by any honest reading of the drug's harms. Here is the same lesson, two centuries earlier and even more stark. Britain's policy toward China was not about public health or pharmacology. It was about the trade balance and corporate profit. The opium was the same molecule we have been tracing since the Sumerians. What changed was who controlled it, and what they wanted from it.

[PICTURE] Picture two ledgers on a table. On one side, silver leaving Britain for Chinese tea. On the other, opium grown in Bengal flowing into China, and the silver flowing back. Between the two ledgers, picture a Chinese commissioner ordering more than 1,200 tons of opium destroyed in 1839, and then picture British warships arriving in answer. The war was not about right and wrong. It was about which way the silver moved.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: Britain ran a trade deficit buying Chinese tea, silk, and porcelain with silver, so the British East India Company sold Bengal-grown opium into China to reverse it, and when Lin Zexu destroyed more than 1,200 tons of opium in 1839 and China tried to stop the trade, Britain fought the First Opium War (1839 to 1842) and Second (1856 to 1860) to force the trade open, taking reparations, ports, and Hong Kong (Lovell, 2011). Drug policy here was driven by economic interest, not public health.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why did Britain go to war rather than let China regulate opium? [Beat] Because the opium trade had reversed Britain's trade deficit with China and was producing large profits, and Britain chose military force over losing that economic advantage (Lovell, 2011).

## Key Takeaways
- Britain bought Chinese tea, silk, and porcelain with silver and drained its reserves because China wanted few British goods, creating a severe trade deficit (Lovell, 2011).
- The British East India Company sold opium grown in Bengal into China to reverse the deficit, producing millions of users and deep social disruption (Lovell, 2011).
- Commissioner Lin Zexu destroyed more than 1,200 tons of confiscated opium in 1839 as China tried to stop the trade (Lovell, 2011).
- Britain fought the First Opium War (1839 to 1842) and Second (1856 to 1860) to force the trade open, taking reparations, ports, and Hong Kong, a stark case of drug policy driven by economic interest rather than public health (Lovell, 2011).

## Sources
- Lovell, J. (2011). The Opium War: Drugs, dreams, and the making of modern China. Picador.

title: Purdue and the Sacklers
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can document how Purdue Pharma's deception helped drive the modern opioid crisis.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we covered the Opium Wars. Two quick questions.
One: why did Britain sell opium to China? [Beat] To reverse a trade deficit; the British East India Company sold Bengal-grown opium into China to turn the flow of silver back in Britain's favor (Lovell, 2011).
Two: what did Lin Zexu do in 1839? [Beat] He confiscated and destroyed more than 1,200 tons of opium and demanded Britain end the trade (Lovell, 2011).

[HOOK] We have watched the false promise of a safe opioid told for five thousand years. In 1996, it was told again, this time by an American company, with a marketing operation behind it and internal documents proving the company knew better. By the time the documents came out, hundreds of thousands of people were dead.

[TEACH] Begin with the claim. In 1996, Purdue Pharma launched OxyContin, an extended-release form of the opioid oxycodone (Keefe, 2021). The core marketing claim was that the extended-release formula made OxyContin significantly less addictive than other opioid pain medications. That is the same old promise: a new opioid, sold as the safe one.
It was false, and Purdue knew it was false. Internal company documents, later revealed through litigation, showed the extended-release mechanism could be defeated by crushing the tablet, and that the drug was highly addictive (Keefe, 2021). The company made a claim of safety while holding evidence of danger. That is not a mistake. That is deception, documented in the record.
Now the marketing operation, because the deception was not just a label. Purdue built a sales force that targeted physicians, especially in rural and economically distressed areas (Keefe, 2021). It tracked prescribing data and built incentives that rewarded the doctors who prescribed the most. And it lobbied against prescription-monitoring programs, the very systems designed to catch dangerous prescribing patterns (Keefe, 2021). Every piece of that machine pushed in the same direction: more pills, more places, fewer checks.
Then the outcome, stated soberly, because these are the numbers that matter. OxyContin generated more than 35 billion dollars in revenue for Purdue between 1996 and 2020 (Keefe, 2021). Over a closely overlapping stretch, from 1999 to 2021, roughly 645,000 people in the United States died from opioid overdoses (Keefe, 2021). Those 645,000 were not a statistic. They were veterans and nurses and teenagers and parents, people often first handed a prescription by a doctor who had been told the drug was safe.
And the accountability. In 2024, the Sackler family, who controlled and profited from Purdue, agreed to a settlement of about 6.5 billion dollars, with no individual criminal convictions of family members (Keefe, 2021). Set that against the harm and the lesson is plain. This is the East India Company pattern wearing a lab coat: profit captured by the company, the human cost pushed onto families and communities. The difference is that this time the deception is documented and the dead are counted.

[PICTURE] Picture a sales map of the United States with the highest-prescribing doctors marked, clustered over rural and distressed counties. Lay over it a revenue line climbing past 35 billion dollars. Lay over that a second number, 645,000, the overdose dead from 1999 to 2021. And at the bottom of the page, one more figure: a 6.5 billion dollar settlement in 2024, signed with no family member ever criminally convicted. Read all four numbers together. That is the shape of the modern crisis.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: Purdue Pharma launched OxyContin in 1996 claiming its extended-release formula was significantly less addictive, while internal documents showed the company knew the formula could be defeated and the drug was highly addictive, so the claim was false; the company targeted physicians in distressed areas, rewarded high prescribers, and lobbied against monitoring programs, taking more than 35 billion dollars in revenue from 1996 to 2020 while roughly 645,000 Americans died of opioid overdoses from 1999 to 2021, and the 2024 Sackler settlement of about 6.5 billion dollars brought no individual criminal convictions of family members (Keefe, 2021).

[CHECK] Quick check. What specific deception did Purdue Pharma commit with OxyContin, and what was the documented scale of the harm? [Beat] Purdue claimed OxyContin's extended-release formula made it significantly less addictive while internal documents showed the company knew it was highly addictive; the drug brought more than 35 billion dollars in revenue while roughly 645,000 Americans died of opioid overdoses from 1999 to 2021 (Keefe, 2021).

## Key Takeaways
- Purdue Pharma launched OxyContin in 1996 with the false claim that its extended-release formula made it significantly less addictive than other opioids (Keefe, 2021).
- Internal documents revealed through litigation showed Purdue knew the formula could be defeated by crushing the tablet and that the drug was highly addictive (Keefe, 2021).
- Purdue targeted physicians in rural and distressed areas, rewarded high-prescribing doctors, and lobbied against prescription-monitoring programs (Keefe, 2021).
- OxyContin brought more than 35 billion dollars in revenue (1996 to 2020); roughly 645,000 Americans died of opioid overdoses (1999 to 2021); the 2024 Sackler settlement of about 6.5 billion dollars brought no individual criminal convictions of family members (Keefe, 2021).

## Sources
- Keefe, P. R. (2021). Empire of pain: The secret history of the Sackler dynasty. Doubleday.

title: Who Profited, Who Paid
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can contrast the profits of the opioid crisis with its vastly larger cost to society.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we covered Purdue and the Sacklers. Two quick questions.
One: what was the false claim Purdue made about OxyContin? [Beat] That it was rarely addictive when used for pain, which was not true (Keefe, 2021).
Two: roughly how many lives were lost from 1999 to 2021? [Beat] About 645,000 (Keefe, 2021).

[HOOK] Here is the question that cuts to the bone of this whole episode. Someone made a fortune off the opioid crisis. Someone else paid the bill. They are not the same people, and the gap between the two numbers is almost too large to feel. Today we put the profit and the cost side by side.

[TEACH] Start with the profit. Purdue Pharma earned more than thirty-five billion dollars in total revenue from OxyContin between 1996 and 2020 (Keefe, 2021). Out of that, the Sackler family, who owned the company, pulled out roughly eleven billion dollars for themselves (Keefe, 2021). That is the upside. A single drug, a single family, eleven billion dollars.
Now the cost. The documented economic cost of the opioid crisis to the United States has been measured at more than one trillion dollars (Florence et al., 2021). And that figure does not include the part no spreadsheet can hold: roughly 645,000 lives lost, each one a person, not a number.
So look at the ratio, because the ratio is the whole story. Purdue's roughly thirty-five billion dollars in revenue, set against more than one trillion dollars in crisis cost, is about one to thirty. For every one dollar the company made, the broader economy paid about thirty dollars. That thirty dollars did not vanish. It landed on hospitals, on families burying their children, on towns paying for it, on governments. Economists have a word for this. It is called externalizing the cost. You keep the profit. You push the cost onto everyone else.
And if that pattern sounds familiar, it should. It is the same structure as the British East India Company's opium trade two centuries ago. The Company captured the profit privately. The cost, addiction and ruin and eventually war, was paid publicly by the people on the other end of the trade. Different century, same machine. Profit captured privately, cost externalized publicly.

[PICTURE] Picture a set of scales. On one side, eleven billion dollars in the Sackler family's hands, the kind of money that buys museum wings with your name on them. On the other side, more than a trillion dollars of damage, and behind it 645,000 empty chairs at kitchen tables. The scales do not balance. They were never meant to. That imbalance was the design.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: Purdue earned more than thirty-five billion dollars from OxyContin and the Sacklers extracted about eleven billion, while the documented cost of the crisis topped one trillion dollars alongside about 645,000 lives lost (Keefe, 2021; Florence et al., 2021). The cost-to-profit ratio is about thirty to one, profit captured privately and cost externalized publicly, the same template as the East India Company's opium trade.

[CHECK] Quick check. What does it mean that the crisis cost was externalized? [Beat] It means the company kept the profit while pushing the cost, about thirty dollars for every dollar earned, onto healthcare systems, families, communities, and governments (Florence et al., 2021).

## Key Takeaways
- Purdue Pharma earned more than thirty-five billion dollars from OxyContin from 1996 to 2020, and the Sackler family extracted roughly eleven billion (Keefe, 2021).
- The documented U.S. economic cost of the crisis exceeded one trillion dollars, with about 645,000 lives lost that no dollar figure can capture (Florence et al., 2021).
- The cost-to-profit ratio is about thirty to one: for every dollar made, the economy paid about thirty.
- The structure matches the British East India Company's opium trade: profit captured privately, cost externalized publicly.

## Sources
- Keefe, P. R. (2021). Empire of pain: The secret history of the Sackler dynasty. Doubleday.
- Florence, C., Luo, F., & Rice, K. (2021). The economic burden of opioid use disorder and fatal opioid overdose in the United States, 2017. Drug and Alcohol Dependence, 218, 108350.

title: Treatment Versus Punishment
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain why treatment and harm reduction are more cost-effective than incarceration.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we covered who profited and who paid. Two quick questions.
One: roughly what was the cost-to-profit ratio of the crisis? [Beat] About thirty to one, thirty dollars in cost for every dollar made (Florence et al., 2021).
Two: what does it mean that a cost was externalized? [Beat] The company kept the profit while pushing the cost onto healthcare systems, families, communities, and governments.

[HOOK] Once the crisis is here, a country has to decide what to do with the people caught in it. You can treat them, or you can punish them. We tend to argue about that choice as if it were only about values. But it is also about money, and the money tells a clear story. Treatment is cheaper, and it works. Punishment is more expensive, and it does not.

[TEACH] Walk through the numbers as public health, plainly. Medication-assisted treatment, where a person gets a medicine that steadies the brain along with support, costs roughly five thousand to fifteen thousand dollars a person a year (National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2023). For that money, opioid use goes down, crime goes down, and deaths go down. When you add up what society saves, the estimated benefit is between twenty-five thousand and one hundred five thousand dollars a year per person treated (National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2023). So you spend a little and you get back a lot. That is a rare thing in any budget.
Now the other path. Incarcerating someone for a drug offense costs roughly thirty-five thousand to forty thousand dollars a year (National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2023). For that larger sum, there is no evidence that the addiction gets better, and relapse after release is high. You spend more, and you get less. The brain disorder is still there when the cell door opens.
And then there is the cheapest tool of all. Naloxone, the medicine that reverses an opioid overdose, costs about forty dollars a dose (National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2023). Forty dollars to reverse an overdose and keep a person alive long enough to have any future at all.
Put it together and the lesson is simple, and it is a public-health lesson, not a moral lecture. Treatment and harm reduction cost far less than punishment and actually reduce harm. So when a system chooses to punish instead of treat, it is not being forced into that choice by economics. It is choosing it. The cheaper, more effective option was sitting right there.

[PICTURE] Picture two doors. Over the first, a sign reads about ten thousand dollars, and behind it people get better and society saves money. Over the second, a sign reads about forty thousand dollars, and behind it the addiction is unchanged. Most of the time, the country has walked through the second door. Not because it was cheaper. Because of how we decided to see the problem.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: medication-assisted treatment costs about five thousand to fifteen thousand dollars a year and returns twenty-five thousand to one hundred five thousand in benefit, while incarceration costs about thirty-five thousand to forty thousand a year with no evidence of reducing addiction, and naloxone reverses an overdose for about forty dollars (National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2023). Punishing instead of treating is a policy choice, not an economic necessity.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why is choosing punishment over treatment a policy choice rather than an economic one? [Beat] Because treatment and harm reduction cost far less and actually reduce use, crime, and death, so the more expensive, less effective option is chosen for other reasons, not forced by the math (National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2023).

If you or someone you love needs help, the panel on this lesson links to the National Institute on Drug Abuse resource on medications that treat opioid use disorder. Treatment works.

## Key Takeaways
- Medication-assisted treatment costs roughly five thousand to fifteen thousand dollars a person a year and returns an estimated twenty-five thousand to one hundred five thousand in societal benefit (National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2023).
- Incarceration for a drug offense costs roughly thirty-five thousand to forty thousand dollars a year with no evidence of reducing addiction and high relapse on release (National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2023).
- Naloxone reverses an overdose and saves a life for about forty dollars a dose (National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2023).
- Treatment and harm reduction cost far less than punishment and work better, so choosing to punish is a policy choice, not an economic necessity.

## Sources
- National Institute on Drug Abuse. (2023). Medications to treat opioid use disorder. https://nida.nih.gov

Useful for the treatment economics lesson.","url":"https://nida.nih.gov/publications/research-reports/medications-to-treat-opioid-addiction/overview","source_url":"National Institute on Drug Abuse"}]

title: The Scott County Lesson
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can use the 2015 Indiana HIV outbreak to explain the cost-benefit case for harm reduction.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we compared treatment and punishment. Two quick questions.
One: roughly what does medication-assisted treatment cost a year versus incarceration? [Beat] About five thousand to fifteen thousand dollars for treatment, against about thirty-five thousand to forty thousand for incarceration (National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2023).
Two: what does naloxone do? [Beat] It reverses an opioid overdose and saves a life for about forty dollars a dose (National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2023).

[HOOK] Sometimes a whole argument plays out in one small town, in real time, with real people. In 2015, in Austin, a town in Scott County, Indiana, the country ran an experiment it never meant to run. The result was the largest rural HIV outbreak in American history. Handle this one with care, because behind every number here is a person.

[TEACH] Here is what happened. In 2015, Austin saw an HIV outbreak with 215 cases, nearly all of them tied to people injecting opioids and sharing needles (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2015). For a small rural town, that is staggering, the largest rural HIV outbreak the country had seen.
Now the economics, carefully, because the point of the numbers is the lesson inside them. The lifetime healthcare cost of caring for those 215 HIV cases comes to roughly eighty-six million dollars. HIV is a lifelong condition, and the care it requires is expensive for decades.
Against that, set the tool that might have prevented it. A needle-exchange program, where people can get clean needles so the virus does not pass from one person to the next, costs on the order of two hundred thousand to four hundred thousand dollars a year to run. Put those two figures next to each other and the gap is enormous. Eighty-six million dollars in lifetime cost, against a prevention program costing a few hundred thousand a year. That is a cost-benefit ratio of about two hundred to one in favor of harm reduction.
And here is the hard part of the lesson. Programs like needle exchange had been blocked in that area on ideological grounds, treated as if helping people inject more safely meant approving of injection. So the choice that was actually made was not the cheaper one and not the safer one. It was the one that fit a belief. The cost of putting ideology ahead of public-health evidence was not abstract. It was 215 people, and roughly eighty-six million dollars. That is the lesson Scott County leaves us with, and it deserves to be carried soberly.

[PICTURE] Picture a small Indiana town. On one side of the scale, a modest clinic handing out clean needles, a few hundred thousand dollars a year. On the other side, 215 neighbors living with a lifelong illness and a bill near eighty-six million dollars. The cheaper, kinder path was known. It was blocked. The expensive, painful one arrived instead.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: in 2015, Austin, in Scott County, Indiana, had an HIV outbreak of 215 cases tied to injectable opioid use, the largest rural HIV outbreak in U.S. history, with a lifetime care cost near eighty-six million dollars, while a needle-exchange program that might have prevented it would have cost a few hundred thousand a year, a cost-benefit ratio of about two hundred to one for harm reduction, blocked on ideological grounds (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2015).

[CHECK] Quick check. What does Scott County show about choosing ideology over public-health evidence? [Beat] That it carried a measurable human and financial cost: 215 HIV cases and roughly eighty-six million dollars, when prevention would have cost about two hundred times less (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2015).

## Key Takeaways
- In 2015, Austin, in Scott County, Indiana, had an HIV outbreak of 215 cases tied to injectable opioid use, the largest rural HIV outbreak in U.S. history (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2015).
- The lifetime healthcare cost of those cases was on the order of eighty-six million dollars.
- A needle-exchange program that might have prevented the outbreak would have cost roughly two hundred thousand to four hundred thousand dollars a year, a cost-benefit ratio of about two hundred to one in favor of harm reduction.
- Such programs had been blocked on ideological grounds, so choosing ideology over public-health evidence carried a measurable human and financial cost.

## Sources
- Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. (2015). Community outbreak of HIV infection linked to injection drug use of oxymorphone, Indiana, 2015. MMWR, 64(16), 443-444.

The honest health picture for this episode.","url":"https://www.cdc.gov/overdose-prevention/about/understanding-the-opioid-overdose-epidemic.html","source_url":"Centers for Disease Control and Prevention"}]

title: Lin Zexu's Letter
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can analyze how Lin Zexu used Britain's own values against its opium policy.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we worked through the economics in Scott County. Two quick questions.
One: roughly what was the cost-benefit ratio of harm reduction in Scott County? [Beat] Roughly 200 to 1, the savings against the cost of prevention.
Two: what did choosing ideology over evidence cost there? [Beat] A delayed needle exchange and a preventable HIV outbreak in a small Indiana town.

[HOOK] In 1839, before a single shot was fired in the Opium War, a Chinese official sat down and wrote a letter to the Queen of England. He did not threaten her. He did not beg. He did something harder. He held up Britain's own values and asked Britain to live by them.

[TEACH] The official was Lin Zexu. China's Qing government appointed him Imperial Commissioner and gave him one job: end the opium trade that was hollowing out the country. His most famous move was not a battle. It was a formal letter to Queen Victoria, written in 1839 (Lin Zexu, 1839).
Listen to his core strategy, because it is the whole lesson. We call it moral pressure. Moral pressure means taking the gap between what someone claims to believe and what they actually do, and naming that gap out loud. Lin Zexu knew that Britain did not allow opium to be sold freely to its own people, because Britain understood opium was harmful. So he asked, in effect, why a country that recognized opium as harmful would still sell it to others for profit. He was not inventing a standard. He was using Britain's standard.
He pressed this with a reciprocity appeal, an argument from fairness. The idea was simple: what you would not want for yourself, do not impose on others. If opium is too dangerous for London, it is too dangerous for Guangzhou. That principle does not need translation. It works in any language and any century (Lin Zexu, 1839).
Then he documented the harm. He did not speak in vague terms about morality. He described opium's specific damage to Chinese society, the men who abandoned their families, the officials who could no longer do their work, the public order coming apart. Specific harm is harder to dismiss than a general complaint.
And notice who he was talking to. He addressed Victoria as an equal sovereign, one ruler to another, and he held her personally accountable. He did not aim his argument at "the British government" as a faceless thing. He aimed it at a named person with the power to choose. Direct address creates personal responsibility. It is much harder to look away when the letter has your name on it.

[PICTURE] Picture the letter itself, formal, careful, addressed to the Queen, crossing an ocean by ship. Now picture it arriving and getting no reply. The argument was sound. The harm was real. The standard was Britain's own. And the answer was silence, and then war.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: Lin Zexu, appointed by China's Qing government to end the opium trade, wrote a formal 1839 letter to Queen Victoria using moral pressure, holding Britain to its own stated value that opium was too harmful to sell at home, joined to a reciprocity appeal, specific documentation of harm, and direct personal address to the Queen as an equal sovereign (Lin Zexu, 1839). The letter got no reply, and Britain went to war.

[CHECK] Here is the real question this lesson leaves open. The letter was ignored and Britain went to war, so did the rhetoric fail? Or does a text succeed when it clearly states the truth, even if its immediate audience refuses to listen? [Beat] There is no single answer, and that is the point. By the measure of stopping the war, the letter failed. By the measure of stating the truth clearly enough that we still read it as evidence almost two centuries later, it did exactly what a strong argument is supposed to do (Lin Zexu, 1839). A text can lose its moment and still win the record.

## Key Takeaways
- Lin Zexu was appointed Imperial Commissioner by China's Qing government and wrote a formal 1839 letter to Queen Victoria to end the opium trade (Lin Zexu, 1839).
- His core strategy was moral pressure: naming the gap between Britain's stated value that opium was harmful and its choice to sell opium to China for profit.
- He added a reciprocity appeal, what you would not want for yourself, do not impose on others, plus specific documentation of harm and direct personal address to the Queen.
- The letter received no reply and Britain went to war, which raises the genuine question of whether ignored rhetoric fails or succeeds by stating the truth.

## Sources
- Lin Zexu. (1839). Letter to Queen Victoria. (Primary source.)

Britain ignored it and went to war.","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lin_Zexu","source_url":"Reference summary with the 1839 letter"}]

title: Whose Story Gets Told
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can analyze the ethics of the addiction narrative as a genre.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we read Lin Zexu's letter. Two quick questions.
One: what does moral pressure mean? [Beat] Taking the gap between what someone claims to believe and what they actually do, and naming it out loud (Lin Zexu, 1839).
Two: what was the genuine question the lesson left open? [Beat] Whether ignored rhetoric fails, or whether a text succeeds by stating the truth clearly even when its audience refuses to listen (Lin Zexu, 1839).

[HOOK] There is a whole kind of book about addiction, usually written in the first person, usually intimate. And every one of them shares a quiet problem that has nothing to do with how well it is written. The problem is who gets to write it at all.

[TEACH] Start with the genre itself. The addiction narrative is a recognizable literary form. It is often first-person, and it holds a hard tension between pleasure and destruction. This is not new. It goes back at least to Thomas De Quincey and his 1821 Confessions of an English Opium-Eater, the first major literary account of addiction in English. De Quincey does both things at once. He romanticizes opium, describing the clarity and the pleasure, and he documents its ruin, the lost years and the dependency that consumed his life (De Quincey, 1821). That tension is the engine of the genre, and it is also the source of its trouble.
Now the ethics. Three problems sit inside this genre, and you should be able to name all three.
First, survivorship bias. The people who write addiction narratives are, by definition, the ones who survived. The voices we never hear are the ones who did not. So the genre quietly skews toward survival and recovery, which is not the whole truth of what addiction does.
Second, the glamorization problem. Can a narrative honestly document pleasure without being read as endorsing it? De Quincey describes ecstasy on the page. A reader can take that description as a warning, or as an invitation. The text alone cannot fully control which (De Quincey, 1821). A story that only shows harm can feel preachy and false; a story that shows pleasure risks reading like recruitment. That is a real tension, not a solved one.
Third, the individual against the structural. The opioid crisis is structural. It runs on corporate marketing, on collapsed local economies, on weak rural healthcare. But the addiction narrative centers single people, one "I" at a time. So how do you tell a structural story in a form built for one voice?
Some writers answer that last question by changing the form. Sam Quinones, in Dreamland, weaves many storylines together, the company, the small towns, the dealers, the families, so that no single person carries the whole crisis; the structure itself argues that you need every vantage point at once (Quinones, 2015). Patrick Radden Keefe does something related in Empire of Pain, using deeply reported narrative nonfiction to trace one family and the system around it (Keefe, 2021). Both reach for the structural account by widening the lens past a single first-person voice.

[PICTURE] Picture a shelf of addiction memoirs, every spine an "I," every author someone who lived to write it. Now picture the empty space beside the shelf, the books that were never written because their authors did not survive. The genre is shaped as much by that empty space as by the books on the shelf.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the addiction narrative is a literary genre, often first-person, holding a tension between pleasure and destruction since De Quincey's 1821 Confessions, and it raises three ethical problems, survivorship bias, the glamorization problem, and the gap between individual and structural; writers like Quinones in Dreamland and Keefe in Empire of Pain answer the structural problem with narrative nonfiction that weaves many perspectives together (De Quincey, 1821; Quinones, 2015; Keefe, 2021).

[CHECK] Quick check. Why is survivorship bias a problem for the addiction narrative as a genre? [Beat] Because the people who write these narratives are, by definition, the ones who survived, so the stories that get told skew toward survival and leave out the voices of those who did not, which distorts what the genre represents (De Quincey, 1821).

## Key Takeaways
- The addiction narrative is a literary genre, often first-person, holding a tension between pleasure and destruction since De Quincey's 1821 Confessions (De Quincey, 1821).
- It raises three ethical problems: survivorship bias, the glamorization problem, and the gap between the individual story and the structural crisis.
- Survivors write these narratives, which skews what gets told; documenting pleasure risks reading as endorsement; and a one-voice form struggles to carry a structural story.
- Quinones in Dreamland and Keefe in Empire of Pain answer the structural problem with narrative nonfiction that weaves many perspectives into one account (Quinones, 2015; Keefe, 2021).

## Sources
- De Quincey, T. (1821). Confessions of an English opium-eater. (Public domain.)
- Quinones, S. (2015). Dreamland: The true tale of America's opiate epidemic. Bloomsbury Press.
- Keefe, P. R. (2021). Empire of pain: The secret history of the Sackler dynasty. Doubleday.

title: Key Terms: Opioids
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can define the core vocabulary from Episode 17 across all four lenses.
Subject: Vocabulary | Run time: about 5 minutes

Your word bank for the Opioids episode. Read it quick, out loud if you can. The full glossary lives in the course Resources. If you or someone you know is struggling, the SAMHSA helpline is 1-800-662-4357, and naloxone reverses an opioid overdose.

## Geography

**Papaver somniferum** (pah-PAH-vehr sahm-NIF-er-um). The opium poppy, source of opium and all natural opiates (Kapoor, 1995).

**Golden Triangle / Golden Crescent.** The two main illicit opium regions (Myanmar, Laos, Thailand; and Afghanistan, Pakistan, Iran), where production follows weak government control (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).

**Fentanyl** (FEN-tuh-nil). A synthetic opioid 50 to 100 times more potent than morphine, the main driver of overdose deaths today (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2023).

**Deaths of despair.** A rise in deaths from drugs, alcohol, and suicide in economically declining communities (Case & Deaton, 2020).

## Social Studies

**Hul gil** (HOOL gil). The Sumerian name for the poppy, meaning the joy plant (Booth, 1996).

**Soldier's disease.** Morphine dependence among roughly 400,000 Civil War veterans treated for pain (Courtwright, 2001).

**OxyContin** (OK-see-kon-tin). Purdue Pharma's extended-release oxycodone, falsely marketed as less addictive (Keefe, 2021).

## Economics

**Externalized cost.** A cost borne by society rather than the producer, like the crisis cost against Purdue's revenue (Florence et al., 2021).

**Naloxone** (nuh-LOK-sohn). The medication, sold as Narcan, that reverses an opioid overdose, about 40 dollars a dose (National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2023).

**MAT.** Medication-assisted treatment for opioid use disorder, which reduces use, overdose, and death (National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2023).

**Harm reduction.** Strategies like naloxone and needle exchange that reduce harm without requiring abstinence.

## English Language Arts

**Moral pressure.** A rhetorical strategy that uses an adversary's own stated values against their actions, as Lin Zexu did (Lin Zexu, 1839).

**False authority.** Claiming expertise not supported by evidence, as in Purdue's misleading medical marketing.

**Survivorship bias.** The skew in addiction narratives toward those who survived to write them.

**Narrative nonfiction.** Journalistic storytelling using novelistic technique, as in Quinones and Keefe (Keefe, 2021).

## Sources
- Booth, M. (1996). Opium: A history. St. Martin's Press.
- Case, A., & Deaton, A. (2020). Deaths of despair and the future of capitalism. Princeton University Press.
- Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. (2023). Understanding the opioid overdose epidemic. https://www.cdc.gov/opioids/
- Courtwright, D. T. (2001). Dark paradise: A history of opiate addiction in America. Harvard University Press.
- Florence, C., Luo, F., & Rice, K. (2021). The economic burden of opioid use disorder and fatal opioid overdose in the United States, 2017. Drug and Alcohol Dependence, 218, 108350.
- Kapoor, L. D. (1995). Opium poppy: Botany, chemistry, and pharmacology. Haworth Press.
- Keefe, P. R. (2021). Empire of pain: The secret history of the Sackler dynasty. Doubleday.
- Lin Zexu. (1839). Letter to Queen Victoria.
- National Institute on Drug Abuse. (2023). Medications to treat opioid use disorder. https://nida.nih.gov
- United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime. (2023). World drug report 2023. https://www.unodc.org

title: Cumulative Review: Opioids
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can recall the top facts from all four lenses and see how they connect.
Subject: Review | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Let's pull the Opioids episode together, one quick fact per lens, then the thread that ties them.

[TEACH] Start with geography. The opium poppy grows where climate suits it and government control is weak, in the Golden Triangle and the Golden Crescent (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023). But the deadliest opioid today, fentanyl, is synthetic, 50 to 100 times stronger than morphine, made from chemicals rather than grown, so crop eradication cannot touch it. And the crisis landed hardest in Appalachia and the Rust Belt, the deaths of despair in communities that had already lost their jobs (Case & Deaton, 2020).

Now social studies. The poppy was the ancient joy plant and a real medicine, but each new opioid was sold as the safe answer to the last, from Bayer's heroin in 1898 to Purdue's OxyContin (Booth, 1996). Britain forced opium on China in the Opium Wars to protect its profits (Lovell, 2011). And Purdue Pharma's documented deception helped kill roughly 645,000 Americans, while the Sacklers settled for about 6.5 billion dollars with no criminal convictions (Keefe, 2021).

Then economics. Purdue made about 35 billion dollars against more than 1 trillion dollars in crisis cost, roughly one to thirty, the cost externalized onto everyone else (Florence et al., 2021). Yet treatment costs far less than incarceration and works, naloxone costs about 40 dollars to reverse an overdose, and Scott County showed harm reduction beating the alternative by about 200 to 1 (National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2023; Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2015).

Last, language. Lin Zexu used moral pressure, holding Britain to its own values, and was ignored (Lin Zexu, 1839). And the addiction-narrative genre, from De Quincey onward, struggles with survivorship bias and the glamorization problem, which writers like Quinones and Keefe answer with structural narrative nonfiction (Keefe, 2021).

[REMEMBER] Here is the thread, held gently. The opioid crisis is the season's clearest proof that profit and policy, not the molecule alone, decide who lives and dies. Geography shows where harm was aimed, history shows the repeated false promise, economics shows that what works costs less than what we do, and language shows whose stories survive. The hopeful truth stands: naloxone reverses overdoses, and treatment works.

[CHECK] One last check before the quiz. What is the hopeful truth this episode insists on? [Beat] That overdoses can be reversed with naloxone and that treatment works, so the crisis is not hopeless (National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2023).

## Key Takeaways
- Geography: the poppy grows where the state is weak, but synthetic fentanyl follows chemistry, and harm concentrated in regions of economic decline (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023; Case & Deaton, 2020).
- Social studies: a real medicine became a repeated false promise, from the Opium Wars to Purdue's documented deception (Lovell, 2011; Keefe, 2021).
- Economics: profit was tiny next to the externalized cost, and treatment and harm reduction cost less and work (Florence et al., 2021; National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2023).
- Language: Lin Zexu's moral pressure was ignored, and the addiction narrative raises hard ethical questions (Lin Zexu, 1839).

## Sources
- Booth, M. (1996). Opium: A history. St. Martin's Press.
- Case, A., & Deaton, A. (2020). Deaths of despair and the future of capitalism. Princeton University Press.
- Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. (2015). Community outbreak of HIV infection linked to injection drug use of oxymorphone, Indiana, 2015. MMWR, 64(16), 443-444.
- Florence, C., Luo, F., & Rice, K. (2021). The economic burden of opioid use disorder and fatal opioid overdose in the United States, 2017. Drug and Alcohol Dependence, 218, 108350.
- Keefe, P. R. (2021). Empire of pain: The secret history of the Sackler dynasty. Doubleday.
- Lin Zexu. (1839). Letter to Queen Victoria.
- Lovell, J. (2011). The Opium War: Drugs, dreams, and the making of modern China. Picador.
- National Institute on Drug Abuse. (2023). Medications to treat opioid use disorder. https://nida.nih.gov
- United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime. (2023). World drug report 2023. https://www.unodc.org

title: Sources and Further Reading: Opioids
text_content:
Every claim in this episode is cited inline so you can check it yourself. Here is the full reference list in APA style. Use it to verify a fact, to dig deeper, or to start your own research for the project. The episode is a public-health and history class, not an endorsement of use. If you or someone you know is struggling with substance use, the SAMHSA national helpline is 1-800-662-4357, free, confidential, and open 24 hours. If someone may be overdosing, call 911; naloxone (Narcan) can reverse an opioid overdose.

## References
