title: Knowledge Check: Opioids
text_content:
This check pulls from all four lenses in Episode 17: geography, social studies, economics, and language.

Each time you take it, you get 10 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here.

This is the hardest topic in the series. A reminder before you begin: this is a public-health and history class, never a how-to. If someone may be overdosing, call 911; naloxone, sold as Narcan, can reverse an opioid overdose and is widely available. For help with substance use, the SAMHSA helpline is free, confidential, and open 24 hours at 1-800-662-4357.

When you finish, take the Project assignment, where the bigger questions, who profited from this crisis and what actually helps, get the long-form treatment they deserve.
quiz_content: {"passingScore":80,"attemptsAllowed":-1,"questionsPerAttempt":10,"shuffleOptions":true,"questions":[{"id":"q1","questionText":"What medication can reverse an opioid overdose, and what should you do first?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q1a","text":"Naloxone (Narcan) can reverse it, and you should call 911 immediately"},{"id":"q1b","text":"Nothing can reverse it"},{"id":"q1c","text":"Coffee, and wait it out"},{"id":"q1d","text":"Another opioid"}],"correctOptionId":"q1a","explanation":"Naloxone, sold as Narcan, can reverse an opioid overdose and is widely available without a prescription. If you think someone is overdosing, call 911 right away.","citation":"Lesson: The Poppy's Promise (intro)"},{"id":"q2","questionText":"Beyond climate, what do the Golden Triangle and Golden Crescent share?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q2a","text":"Weak or absent central government control, which shelters illicit production"},{"id":"q2b","text":"Strong central governments"},{"id":"q2c","text":"Tropical rainforest"},{"id":"q2d","text":"Coastal ports"}],"correctOptionId":"q2a","explanation":"Both regions combine a suitable climate with weak state control, because illicit growing needs the shelter of limited enforcement.","citation":"Lesson: Where the Poppy Grows"},{"id":"q3","questionText":"What happened to opium production after Afghanistan's 2023 Taliban ban?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q3a","text":"Afghan production fell about 95 percent, and production shifted to Myanmar while demand held steady"},{"id":"q3b","text":"All opium production ended worldwide"},{"id":"q3c","text":"Demand fell to zero"},{"id":"q3d","text":"Production doubled in Afghanistan"}],"correctOptionId":"q3a","explanation":"Afghan production dropped about 95 percent, so Myanmar became the world's largest producer. The crop moved to the next ungoverned space; demand did not change.","citation":"Lesson: Where the Poppy Grows"},{"id":"q4","questionText":"Why is fentanyl harder to disrupt than plant-based opioids?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q4a","text":"Its geography is industrial, following chemical supply chains and criminal networks rather than soil and climate"},{"id":"q4b","text":"It grows only in one field"},{"id":"q4c","text":"It is harmless"},{"id":"q4d","text":"It cannot be transported"}],"correctOptionId":"q4a","explanation":"Fentanyl is synthetic, so its map runs from chemical precursors in China to synthesis in Mexico to the U.S. border. You cannot eradicate a chemical reaction with crop spraying.","citation":"Lesson: The Fentanyl Map"},{"id":"q5","questionText":"How potent is fentanyl compared to morphine?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q5a","text":"About 50 to 100 times more potent"},{"id":"q5b","text":"About the same"},{"id":"q5c","text":"Much weaker"},{"id":"q5d","text":"Exactly twice as strong"}],"correctOptionId":"q5a","explanation":"Fentanyl is roughly 50 to 100 times more potent than morphine, which is why a tiny amount can be deadly and why it now drives most overdose deaths.","citation":"Lesson: The Fentanyl Map"},{"id":"q6","questionText":"What creates the enormous price markup on illegal opioids?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q6a","text":"Criminalization itself, which turns a few thousand dollars a kilogram into over a million on the street"},{"id":"q6b","text":"The chemistry of the drug"},{"id":"q6c","text":"High farming costs"},{"id":"q6d","text":"Government subsidies"}],"correctOptionId":"q6a","explanation":"The huge margin is a feature of the law that bans the drug, not of the chemical, since prohibition creates the risk premium that drives the markup.","citation":"Lesson: The Fentanyl Map"},{"id":"q7","questionText":"What are deaths of despair, and how do they relate to opioid geography?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q7a","text":"A rise in deaths from drugs, alcohol, and suicide in economically declining communities, exactly where the crisis hit hardest"},{"id":"q7b","text":"Deaths only among the wealthy"},{"id":"q7c","text":"A type of medication"},{"id":"q7d","text":"Deaths unrelated to the economy"}],"correctOptionId":"q7a","explanation":"Case and Deaton named the pattern deaths of despair: drugs, alcohol, and suicide rising in hollowed-out communities, the same map as the worst opioid harm.","citation":"Lesson: Where the Harm Concentrated"},{"id":"q8","questionText":"How did Purdue Pharma's marketing relate to economically distressed areas?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q8a","text":"It tracked prescription data and deliberately targeted distressed rural areas with injury and few healthcare options"},{"id":"q8b","text":"It avoided poor areas"},{"id":"q8c","text":"It did no marketing"},{"id":"q8d","text":"It only sold in cities"}],"correctOptionId":"q8a","explanation":"Purdue concentrated OxyContin marketing in exactly the distressed communities most vulnerable to it. The crisis there was not random; it was aimed.","citation":"Lesson: Where the Harm Concentrated"},{"id":"q9","questionText":"What did the Sumerians call the opium poppy?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q9a","text":"Hul gil, the joy plant, around 3400 BCE"},{"id":"q9b","text":"OxyContin"},{"id":"q9c","text":"Fentanyl"},{"id":"q9d","text":"They never used it"}],"correctOptionId":"q9a","explanation":"The Sumerians cultivated the poppy around 3400 BCE and called it hul gil, the joy plant, the oldest documented medicinal use.","citation":"Lesson: From Joy Plant to Medicine"},{"id":"q10","questionText":"What false claim did Bayer make about heroin in 1898?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q10a","text":"That it was a non-addictive substitute for morphine, which was untrue"},{"id":"q10b","text":"That it cured cancer"},{"id":"q10c","text":"That it was a food"},{"id":"q10d","text":"Nothing; Bayer never sold it"}],"correctOptionId":"q10a","explanation":"Bayer marketed heroin as a non-addictive substitute for morphine, the first version of a false promise repeated across opioid history.","citation":"Lesson: From Joy Plant to Medicine"},{"id":"q11","questionText":"What is the recurring theme across opioid history?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q11a","text":"Each new opioid was sold as the safe answer to the last one's addiction, and that promise was repeatedly false"},{"id":"q11b","text":"Opioids became safer over time"},{"id":"q11c","text":"No one ever claimed they were safe"},{"id":"q11d","text":"They were always illegal"}],"correctOptionId":"q11a","explanation":"From Bayer's heroin to Purdue's OxyContin, a new opioid was repeatedly marketed as the non-addictive solution to the previous one, and the promise kept failing.","citation":"Lesson: From Joy Plant to Medicine"},{"id":"q12","questionText":"Why did Britain sell opium to China?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q12a","text":"To reverse a trade deficit, since Britain was draining silver buying Chinese tea, silk, and porcelain"},{"id":"q12b","text":"To improve Chinese health"},{"id":"q12c","text":"Because China asked for it"},{"id":"q12d","text":"There was no opium trade"}],"correctOptionId":"q12a","explanation":"China wanted few British goods, draining British silver, so the East India Company sold Bengal opium to China to fix the trade imbalance.","citation":"Lesson: The Opium Wars"},{"id":"q13","questionText":"What did Lin Zexu do in 1839, and how did Britain respond?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q13a","text":"He destroyed more than 1,200 tons of confiscated opium, and Britain went to war rather than accept the regulation"},{"id":"q13b","text":"He sold more opium"},{"id":"q13c","text":"He surrendered immediately"},{"id":"q13d","text":"Britain apologized"}],"correctOptionId":"q13a","explanation":"Commissioner Lin Zexu destroyed over 1,200 tons of opium, and Britain launched the Opium Wars, eventually forcing China to accept the trade and cede Hong Kong.","citation":"Lesson: The Opium Wars"},{"id":"q14","questionText":"What does the Opium Wars example demonstrate about drug policy?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q14a","text":"That drug policy can be driven by economic and political interest rather than public health"},{"id":"q14b","text":"That it is always based on science"},{"id":"q14c","text":"That trade never affects policy"},{"id":"q14d","text":"That China started the war"}],"correctOptionId":"q14a","explanation":"Britain forced a harmful drug trade to protect its profits, an early, stark case of drug policy serving economic interest over public health.","citation":"Lesson: The Opium Wars"},{"id":"q15","questionText":"What did Purdue Pharma falsely claim about OxyContin?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q15a","text":"That its extended-release formula made it significantly less addictive, while internal documents showed the company knew otherwise"},{"id":"q15b","text":"That it was a vitamin"},{"id":"q15c","text":"That it was free"},{"id":"q15d","text":"Nothing; it made no claims"}],"correctOptionId":"q15a","explanation":"Purdue marketed OxyContin as less addictive, but internal documents showed the company knew the formula could be defeated and that the drug was highly addictive.","citation":"Lesson: Purdue and the Sacklers"},{"id":"q16","questionText":"What was the documented outcome of the OxyContin campaign?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q16a","text":"More than 35 billion dollars in revenue, roughly 645,000 overdose deaths, and a 2024 settlement with no individual criminal convictions"},{"id":"q16b","text":"No deaths and a full prison sentence"},{"id":"q16c","text":"A small fine and no harm"},{"id":"q16d","text":"The company was praised"}],"correctOptionId":"q16a","explanation":"OxyContin earned over 35 billion dollars, the crisis claimed about 645,000 lives from 1999 to 2021, and the 2024 Sackler settlement of about 6.5 billion came with no individual criminal convictions.","citation":"Lesson: Purdue and the Sacklers"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"What is the ratio of Purdue's revenue to the crisis cost?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"About one to thirty: for every dollar Purdue made, the economy paid about thirty in costs"},{"id":"q17b","text":"About one to one"},{"id":"q17c","text":"Purdue paid all the costs"},{"id":"q17d","text":"There were no costs"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"Purdue's roughly 35 billion dollars in revenue against more than 1 trillion dollars in crisis cost is about one to thirty, with the cost externalized onto society.","citation":"Lesson: Who Profited, Who Paid"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"What does it mean that the opioid crisis cost was externalized?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q18a","text":"The profit was captured privately while the cost was borne by families, communities, and governments"},{"id":"q18b","text":"The company paid every cost"},{"id":"q18c","text":"There was no cost to anyone"},{"id":"q18d","text":"The cost was paid by other countries only"}],"correctOptionId":"q18a","explanation":"Like the East India Company's opium trade, the gains went to the producer while the enormous costs fell on the public, the definition of an externalized cost.","citation":"Lesson: Who Profited, Who Paid"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"How does medication-assisted treatment compare to incarceration in cost and effect?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"Treatment costs less (about 5,000 to 15,000 dollars a year) and reduces use and death, while incarceration costs more (about 35,000 to 40,000) with no evidence of reducing addiction"},{"id":"q19b","text":"Incarceration is cheaper and more effective"},{"id":"q19c","text":"They cost and work the same"},{"id":"q19d","text":"Neither has any effect"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"Treatment is both cheaper and effective, while incarceration costs more and does not reduce addiction, so choosing punishment is a policy choice, not an economic necessity.","citation":"Lesson: Treatment Versus Punishment"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"How much does a dose of naloxone cost, and what does it do?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"About 40 dollars, and it reverses an opioid overdose to save a life"},{"id":"q20b","text":"Thousands of dollars, and it does nothing"},{"id":"q20c","text":"It is not available"},{"id":"q20d","text":"About 40 dollars, but it causes overdose"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"Naloxone costs around 40 dollars a dose and reverses an opioid overdose immediately, a strikingly cheap and effective harm-reduction tool.","citation":"Lesson: Treatment Versus Punishment"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"What did the 2015 Scott County, Indiana HIV outbreak show about harm reduction?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"Preventing the 215 HIV cases would have cost far less than treating them, about a 200 to 1 cost-benefit for harm reduction"},{"id":"q21b","text":"Harm reduction is more expensive"},{"id":"q21c","text":"There was no outbreak"},{"id":"q21d","text":"Nothing could have helped"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"The 215 cases cost about 86 million dollars in lifetime care, while a needle-exchange program that might have prevented them would have cost a few hundred thousand a year, roughly 200 to 1 in favor of harm reduction.","citation":"Lesson: The Scott County Lesson"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"What broader lesson does Scott County teach about drug policy?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"Choosing ideology over public-health evidence carried a measurable human and financial cost"},{"id":"q22b","text":"Ideology always saves money"},{"id":"q22c","text":"Evidence does not matter"},{"id":"q22d","text":"Outbreaks cannot be prevented"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"Harm-reduction programs had been blocked on ideological grounds, so the outbreak shows that ignoring the evidence cost both lives and money.","citation":"Lesson: The Scott County Lesson"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"What was Lin Zexu's main rhetorical strategy in his 1839 letter?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"Moral pressure: using Britain's own stated values against its actions"},{"id":"q23b","text":"Threatening invasion"},{"id":"q23c","text":"Offering money"},{"id":"q23d","text":"Saying nothing"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"Lin Zexu used moral pressure, asking why a country that recognized opium as harmful would still sell it to others, holding Britain to its own principles.","citation":"Lesson: Lin Zexu's Letter"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"What genuine question does Lin Zexu's ignored letter raise?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"Whether rhetoric fails when ignored, or succeeds by clearly stating the truth even if the audience does not listen"},{"id":"q24b","text":"Whether letters should be written at all"},{"id":"q24c","text":"Whether China should have sold opium"},{"id":"q24d","text":"Nothing worth asking"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"The letter was ignored and Britain went to war, raising the question of whether a text that clearly states the truth still succeeds even when its immediate audience dismisses it.","citation":"Lesson: Lin Zexu's Letter"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"What is survivorship bias in the addiction narrative genre?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"The people who write these narratives are, by definition, the ones who survived, which skews which stories get told"},{"id":"q25b","text":"A medical treatment"},{"id":"q25c","text":"A type of poppy"},{"id":"q25d","text":"A marketing strategy"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"Because addiction memoirs are written by survivors, the genre systematically leaves out the voices of those who did not live to tell their story.","citation":"Lesson: Whose Story Gets Told"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"What is the glamorization problem in addiction narrative?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"Whether a narrative can document the pleasure of a drug without being read as endorsing it"},{"id":"q26b","text":"Whether to use big words"},{"id":"q26c","text":"Whether to charge money for books"},{"id":"q26d","text":"Whether drugs are illegal"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"As far back as De Quincey, the genre holds a tension between describing pleasure and showing destruction, raising the risk that documenting one reads as endorsing it.","citation":"Lesson: Whose Story Gets Told"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"How do writers like Quinones and Keefe handle the structural nature of the crisis?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"They use narrative nonfiction to weave many perspectives into a structural account, not just one person's story"},{"id":"q27b","text":"They ignore the crisis"},{"id":"q27c","text":"They tell only one individual's story"},{"id":"q27d","text":"They write fiction only"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"Dreamland and Empire of Pain use reported narrative nonfiction to braid corporate, community, and family threads into a structural picture of a structural crisis.","citation":"Lesson: Whose Story Gets Told"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"What was 'soldier's disease'?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"Morphine dependence among roughly 400,000 Civil War veterans treated for pain"},{"id":"q28b","text":"A battlefield infection"},{"id":"q28c","text":"A modern fentanyl term"},{"id":"q28d","text":"A type of poppy"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"Heavy morphine use for pain in the American Civil War left roughly 400,000 veterans dependent, a pattern called soldier's disease, showing legitimate medical use can still cause addiction.","citation":"Lesson: From Joy Plant to Medicine"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"Why is this episode treated with such care?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"Because it covers a crisis that killed about 645,000 people, treated as people, not statistics, never as a how-to"},{"id":"q29b","text":"Because opioids are harmless"},{"id":"q29c","text":"Because it teaches drug use"},{"id":"q29d","text":"There is no reason for care"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"The episode is a public-health and history class honoring roughly 645,000 lost lives, emphasizing harm reduction and treatment, and never offering usage guidance.","citation":"Lesson: The Poppy's Promise (intro)"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"Who isolated morphine, and when?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"Friedrich Serturner, in 1804, the first pharmaceutical isolation of a plant compound"},{"id":"q30b","text":"Purdue Pharma, in 1996"},{"id":"q30c","text":"The Sumerians, in 3400 BCE"},{"id":"q30d","text":"Bayer, in 1898"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"Friedrich Serturner isolated morphine from opium in 1804, the first time a plant's active compound was pharmaceutically isolated.","citation":"Lesson: From Joy Plant to Medicine"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"What structural pattern does Purdue Pharma share with the British East India Company?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"Profit captured privately while the costs of addiction were externalized onto society"},{"id":"q31b","text":"Both gave their profits away"},{"id":"q31c","text":"Both were government charities"},{"id":"q31d","text":"They have nothing in common"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"Both captured private profit from an addictive product while pushing the human and financial costs onto the public, the same architecture centuries apart.","citation":"Lesson: Who Profited, Who Paid"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"What is harm reduction?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"Strategies like naloxone and needle exchange that reduce the harm of drug use without requiring abstinence"},{"id":"q32b","text":"A way to sell more drugs"},{"id":"q32c","text":"A type of prison"},{"id":"q32d","text":"A marketing term"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"Harm reduction includes naloxone distribution and needle exchange, which save lives and prevent disease while connecting people to treatment, all without demanding abstinence first.","citation":"Lesson: Treatment Versus Punishment"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"Why does crop eradication fail against the modern opioid supply?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"Because the deadliest opioid, fentanyl, is made in labs from chemicals, not grown in fields"},{"id":"q33b","text":"Because all opioids are legal"},{"id":"q33c","text":"Because there is no supply"},{"id":"q33d","text":"Because eradication is illegal"}],"correctOptionId":"q33a","explanation":"Spraying or burning poppy fields does nothing to fentanyl, which is synthesized industrially, so the strategy that targeted plant drugs no longer fits the threat.","citation":"Lesson: The Fentanyl Map"},{"id":"q34","questionText":"How did Thomas De Quincey's 1821 Confessions treat opium?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q34a","text":"It both romanticized opium's pleasures and documented its destruction"},{"id":"q34b","text":"It only warned against it"},{"id":"q34c","text":"It never mentioned opium"},{"id":"q34d","text":"It was a marketing brochure"}],"correctOptionId":"q34a","explanation":"De Quincey's memoir holds both sides at once, the appeal and the ruin, which is the central tension of the entire addiction-narrative genre.","citation":"Lesson: Whose Story Gets Told"},{"id":"q35","questionText":"What does the Egyptian Ebers Papyrus tell us about opium?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q35a","text":"It documented opium as a medicine around 1550 BCE, evidence of widespread ancient use"},{"id":"q35b","text":"It banned opium"},{"id":"q35c","text":"It is a modern document"},{"id":"q35d","text":"It never mentions opium"}],"correctOptionId":"q35a","explanation":"The Ebers Papyrus, from around 1550 BCE, records opium as a medicine, showing how ancient and widespread its medical use was.","citation":"Lesson: From Joy Plant to Medicine"},{"id":"q36","questionText":"What is the hopeful truth the episode emphasizes?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q36a","text":"Overdoses can be reversed with naloxone, and treatment works"},{"id":"q36b","text":"Nothing can be done"},{"id":"q36c","text":"Opioids are safe"},{"id":"q36d","text":"The crisis is over"}],"correctOptionId":"q36a","explanation":"Alongside the loss, the episode emphasizes that naloxone reverses overdoses and that treatment and harm reduction save lives, the path toward what actually helps.","citation":"Lesson: The Poppy's Promise (intro)"}]}
